CovCombR: Combining Incomplete Covariance Matrices

Deniz Akdemir

2026-06-30

CovCombR in One Example

The CovCombR package combines several incomplete covariance matrices (each covering different subsets of variables) into a single, coherent estimate. The default model automatically selects an appropriate factor structure via BIC, which handles cases where some variable pairs are never observed together in the same study.

The Identifiability Problem

Before fitting, it is important to understand when a combined estimate is identified — meaning the data uniquely determine the answer rather than just reflecting an arbitrary starting value.

With the unconstrained (free-Σ) model, a covariance σ_ij is identifiable only if variables i and j are jointly observed in at least one study. If they never appear together, σ_ij is absent from the likelihood and the estimate is initialization-dependent.

With a factor model (Σ = ΛΛᵀ + Ψ), this constraint is relaxed: even if variables i and j are never jointly observed, their loadings Λ[i,·] and Λ[j,·] can both be identified through their relationships with shared overlapping variables, so σ_ij = Λ[i,·]·Λ[j,·] becomes identified.

The default n_factors = "auto" fits factor models of increasing complexity alongside the free model and selects the best by BIC, giving you the most parsimonious identifiable representation of the data.

Scenario: Block-Structured Covariance with Overlapping Samples

We simulate a covariance matrix with two correlated blocks. Ten independent studies each observe a different overlapping subset of variables. The study patterns deliberately include some pairs never jointly observed, which makes the free-Σ model non-identifiable for those pairs.

set.seed(1234)
p <- 8
var_names <- paste0("Var", seq_len(p))

# True covariance with block structure and heterogeneous variances
block <- matrix(0.6, 4, 4); diag(block) <- 1
true_cov <- matrix(0, p, p)
true_cov[1:4, 1:4] <- block
true_cov[5:8, 5:8] <- block
true_cov[1:4, 5:8] <- 0.2; true_cov[5:8, 1:4] <- 0.2
diag(true_cov) <- 1
std_dev <- c(1.5, 1.2, 1.8, 1.4, 2.0, 1.6, 1.3, 1.9)
true_cov <- diag(std_dev) %*% true_cov %*% diag(std_dev)
dimnames(true_cov) <- list(var_names, var_names)

# Observation patterns — sparse enough that some pairs are never jointly observed
patterns <- list(
  sample1  = var_names[c(1, 2)],
  sample2  = var_names[c(3, 4)],
  sample3  = var_names[c(5, 6)],
  sample4  = var_names[c(7, 8)],
  sample5  = var_names[c(1, 3, 5)],
  sample6  = var_names[c(2, 4, 6)],
  sample7  = var_names[c(3, 5, 7)],
  sample8  = var_names[c(2, 6, 8)],
  sample9  = var_names[c(1, 4, 7)],
  sample10 = var_names[c(2, 5, 8)]
)
nu <- setNames(sample(70:110, length(patterns), replace = TRUE), names(patterns))

S_list <- lapply(names(patterns), function(nm) {
  vars <- patterns[[nm]]
  W_block <- rWishart(1, nu[[nm]], true_cov[vars, vars])[,,1]
  S_block <- W_block / nu[[nm]]
  dimnames(S_block) <- list(vars, vars)
  S_block
})
names(S_list) <- names(patterns)

Naive Stitching Leaves Large Gaps

A common first attempt averages overlapping blocks wherever they exist and leaves NAs elsewhere.

cov_sum    <- matrix(0, p, p, dimnames = list(var_names, var_names))
pair_counts <- matrix(0, p, p, dimnames = list(var_names, var_names))

for (nm in names(S_list)) {
  vars <- rownames(S_list[[nm]])
  cov_sum[vars, vars]    <- cov_sum[vars, vars]    + S_list[[nm]]
  pair_counts[vars, vars] <- pair_counts[vars, vars] + 1
}

naive_cov <- matrix(NA, p, p, dimnames = list(var_names, var_names))
observed_pairs <- pair_counts > 0
naive_cov[observed_pairs] <- cov_sum[observed_pairs] / pair_counts[observed_pairs]

CovCombR Recovers the Missing Structure

The default call uses n_factors = "auto", which selects the best factor model by BIC and ensures an identifiable estimate even for variable pairs never jointly observed.

fit <- fit_covcomb(S_list, nu, se_method = "none")

fit$n_factors
## [1] 2
fit$ic_table
##   n_factors    loglik n_params      AIC      BIC selected
## 1         1 -304.3857       16 640.7714 717.5919    FALSE
## 2         2 -217.6656       23 481.3313 591.7608     TRUE
## 3         3 -226.8419       29 511.6837 650.9209    FALSE
## 4         4 -221.9458       34 511.8916 675.1353    FALSE
## 5        NA -214.6224       36 501.2448 674.0910    FALSE
Sigma_hat <- coef(fit)[var_names, var_names]
S_hat     <- fitted(fit)[var_names, var_names]
em_cov    <- S_hat

The ic_table shows all candidate models (rows) with their BIC values, so you can see exactly why the selected factor count was chosen. The selected model’s Sigma_hat is an identifiable estimate of the full covariance structure.

Visual Comparison: True vs. Naive vs. CovCombR

get_plot_data <- function(mat, label) {
  as.data.frame(mat) |>
    rownames_to_column("row") |>
    tidyr::pivot_longer(-row, names_to = "col", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(matrix = label)
}

plot_data <- bind_rows(
  get_plot_data(true_cov, "True"),
  get_plot_data(naive_cov, "Naive"),
  get_plot_data(em_cov, "CovCombR")
)

plot_data$row    <- factor(plot_data$row,    levels = rev(var_names))
plot_data$col    <- factor(plot_data$col,    levels = var_names)
plot_data$matrix <- factor(plot_data$matrix, levels = c("True", "Naive", "CovCombR"))

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = col, y = row, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(is.na(value), "", sprintf("%.2f", value))),
            size = 2.5, na.rm = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(midpoint = 0, na.value = "#2F2F2F",
                       low = "#2166AC", high = "#B2182B", mid = "#F7F7F7",
                       name = "Covariance") +
  facet_wrap(~matrix, nrow = 1) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        legend.position = "bottom")

CovCombR reconstructs the full block pattern, while the naive stitch lacks estimates for any variable pair never observed together (grey tiles).

Correlation Comparison

true_corr <- cov2cor(true_cov)
em_corr   <- cov2cor(em_cov)
naive_corr <- naive_cov
for (i in 1:p) {
  for (j in 1:p) {
    if (!is.na(naive_cov[i, i]) && !is.na(naive_cov[j, j]) && !is.na(naive_cov[i, j])) {
      naive_corr[i, j] <- naive_cov[i, j] / sqrt(naive_cov[i, i] * naive_cov[j, j])
    }
  }
}

corr_plot_data <- bind_rows(
  get_plot_data(true_corr,  "True"),
  get_plot_data(naive_corr, "Naive"),
  get_plot_data(em_corr,    "CovCombR")
)
corr_plot_data$row    <- factor(corr_plot_data$row,    levels = rev(var_names))
corr_plot_data$col    <- factor(corr_plot_data$col,    levels = var_names)
corr_plot_data$matrix <- factor(corr_plot_data$matrix, levels = c("True", "Naive", "CovCombR"))

ggplot(corr_plot_data, aes(x = col, y = row, fill = value)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(is.na(value), "", sprintf("%.2f", value))),
            size = 2.5, na.rm = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(midpoint = 0, na.value = "#2F2F2F",
                       low = "#2166AC", high = "#B2182B", mid = "#F7F7F7",
                       name = "Correlation") +
  facet_wrap(~matrix, nrow = 1) +
  coord_equal() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 8) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        strip.text = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1),
        legend.position = "bottom")

Recovery Quality in Numbers

upper_tri        <- upper.tri(true_cov, diag = TRUE)
observed_tri     <- observed_pairs & upper_tri
unobserved_tri   <- (!observed_pairs) & upper_tri

coverage_pct <- round(100 * sum(observed_tri) / sum(upper_tri), 1)

stats_tbl <- tibble(
  Estimator = c("Naive stitch", "CovCombR", "CovCombR"),
  Pairs     = c("Observed Only", "Observed Only", "Unobserved Only"),
  RMSE = c(
    sqrt(mean((naive_cov[observed_tri] - true_cov[observed_tri])^2, na.rm = TRUE)),
    sqrt(mean((em_cov[observed_tri]   - true_cov[observed_tri])^2)),
    sqrt(mean((em_cov[unobserved_tri] - true_cov[unobserved_tri])^2))
  ),
  MAE = c(
    mean(abs(naive_cov[observed_tri] - true_cov[observed_tri]), na.rm = TRUE),
    mean(abs(em_cov[observed_tri]   - true_cov[observed_tri])),
    mean(abs(em_cov[unobserved_tri] - true_cov[unobserved_tri]))
  )
) |> mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~round(.x, 3)))

knitr::kable(stats_tbl, caption = "Recovery accuracy on the covariance scale.")
Recovery accuracy on the covariance scale.
Estimator Pairs RMSE MAE
Naive stitch Observed Only 0.251 0.217
CovCombR Observed Only 0.235 0.191
CovCombR Unobserved Only 0.485 0.374

Only 75% of covariance pairs are observable with naive stitching. CovCombR is more accurate than the naive estimator even on those observed pairs, and it supplies low-error estimates for the remaining 25% of previously unobserved pairs.

Overriding the Automatic Selection

The auto selection is appropriate for most situations. When you want explicit control:

# Fit a specific 2-factor model
fit_2f <- fit_covcomb(S_list, nu, n_factors = 2, se_method = "none")

# Fit the unconstrained free-Sigma model (requires all pairs observed)
fit_free <- fit_covcomb(S_list, nu, n_factors = NULL, se_method = "none")

# Use AIC instead of BIC for auto selection
fit_aic <- fit_covcomb(S_list, nu,
                       n_factors = "auto",
                       control = list(ic = "AIC"),
                       se_method = "none")

When to use the free model (n_factors = NULL): If your study design ensures every variable pair is jointly observed in at least one study, the free model is valid and places no factor-structure assumption on the data.

When to specify a fixed k: If you have domain knowledge about the number of latent factors (e.g., a known number of principal components), use that directly.
